Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, but I tried to make it work with what I had. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend time creating diagrams and color-coded notes when I could focus. With only about 16 hours a week to study and limited resources, I had to be strategic about when and where I studied. I usually studied late at night when my living situation was quieter, which sometimes meant I was exhausted during actual class times.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. When deadlines hit, I'd cram everything into intense sessions. Some weeks I barely touched anything because I was dealing with stress or other responsibilities that come with not having much financial cushion. I tried to be consistent, but life kept throwing curveballs at me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification about new content that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was something I missed since my attendance wasn't great. The gamified tasks actually motivated me to log in more often because earning those points or badges gave me something concrete to work toward. Fear of falling behind was honestly a big motivator too.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks the gamified elements made logging in feel almost like a habit. I'd want to check my progress, see if I unlocked anything new, or compare where I stood. It became less of a chore and more like checking a game. The discussion boards also pulled me back in because I'd get curious about what other students were saying.
Q5: The video content was huge for me since I learn best visually. I watched over an hour of videos because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with peers helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. The gamified tasks kept me motivated when my anxiety was telling me to give up.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the longer reading materials or PDFs because they felt overwhelming and I didn't always have time to print them or read on a small screen. Some of the supplementary resources felt optional, so when I was stressed, those were the first things I cut. I also delayed anything that felt like it wouldn't directly impact my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate progress or understand how it connected to assignments or exams. The gamified elements helped a lot because I could see tangible rewards for my effort. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, my anxious brain would tell me to skip it and focus on survival mode.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford to spend time on. When you're stressed and scared about passing, required work takes priority. But sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting or helpful, which was frustrating. I'd feel guilty not doing them but also anxious about spending time on things that wouldn't directly save my grade.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them offline because my internet access isn't always reliable and I needed to conserve data. I'd screenshot diagrams, save videos when possible, and review them without being connected. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what the course offered since I couldn't afford textbooks.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or studying from downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd create visual study aids, mind maps, and diagrams on paper that helped me understand complex topics. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with friends in person or over text, which was real learning but invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, definitely. I'd access a video or PDF once, download or screenshot what I needed, and then study from that copy multiple times. With limited resources and sometimes spotty internet, this was just practical. The LMS would show one view, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times offline.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods when my anxiety spiked and I felt paralyzed. When stress hit a 1 out of 5 level, which is really high for me, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because logging in reminded me of everything I was behind on. Financial stress and feeling scared about my future made it hard to focus on academics. Those were the times my attendance really suffered.
Q13: Workload and competing responsibilities hit me hard. With low resources, I sometimes had to prioritize other things over studying. When I was fatigued from stress and anxiety, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about complex topics made me avoid certain modules until I absolutely had to face them.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed any of it because I was too anxious. The LMS would log my time, but I wasn't really mentally present. It's like my body was there but my brain was fighting its own battles.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible sometimes, like this wall I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel just as scared. It wasn't laziness; it was being paralyzed by fear of failing or not being good enough.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd kick into high gear when something was due, which explains why my assignment scores were actually really good despite low completion rates. The pressure of a deadline could cut through my anxiety and force me to act. Without deadlines, I'd drift and avoid things.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me. When I got positive feedback on an assignment, it boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. But waiting for feedback was stressful, and negative feedback could send me into a spiral. The gamified feedback was actually easier to handle because it felt less personal and more like progress in a game.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but sometimes the workload felt overwhelming given everything else in my life. The gamified elements gave the structure some flexibility and made it feel less rigid. When structure was too loose, I'd lose track; when it was too strict, I'd feel suffocated.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier because it gave me small wins when everything else felt like a struggle. Visual content was easier for me to engage with than walls of text. What made it harder was when there were too many things due at once or when instructions weren't clear. Unclear expectations plus high anxiety is a terrible combination.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in, trying to engage, but getting overwhelmed before they can actually complete and submit work. You might be reading, watching, participating in discussions, but then the final step of submitting feels too scary or you run out of time. High activity doesn't always mean high output when anxiety is involved.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me in some ways. My exam score was decent even though my attendance was low, because I was studying offline a lot. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need to spend hours on the LMS to understand material. Or they might have other resources and strategies that work better for them than the online system.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning happening offline. They can't measure understanding, emotional state, or effort. Someone could look disengaged on paper but be fighting hard behind the scenes, like me.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning and putting in effort, even when it's scary or hard. For me, it's showing up mentally even when I can't always show up physically. It's participating in discussions, asking questions, and trying to understand rather than just memorize. Engagement is about the struggle and persistence, not just the metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much fear I pushed through just to complete assignments or how many times I almost gave up but didn't. It wouldn't capture the offline studying, the visual notes I created, or the peer conversations that helped me learn. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite being scared and stressed with limited resources, I kept climbing and passed the course.
